channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Their Phones Were Stolen in London. Then the Threats Started.
item.description: Tens of thousands of smartphones were reported stolen in the British capital in recent years. For some victims, losing their phone was only the beginning.
item.dc:creator: Lizzie Dearden and Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: Joshua Lott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alex Pikula at home in Chicago. He had his phone stolen while on a trip to London in May 2024, after which his mother started receiving strange and increasingly threatening texts.

item.title: ‘Amazon.com of South Korea’ Is New Source of Tension With U.S.
item.description: A corporate compliance dispute between South Korea’s government and the online retailer Coupang is testing the resilience of U.S.-South Korean ties.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun and Aaron Krolik
item.media:description: President Trump and the president of South Korea, Lee Jae Myung, last year in Gyeongju, South Korea.

item.title: Australia’s ‘Prettiest Street’ Is Grappling With TikTok and Instagram Fame
item.description: For many years, Tasman Drive in Gerringong was a secret held by locals. Now residents must coexist with tourists hungry for the perfect shot.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung and Matthew Abbott
item.media:description: Mayor Cameron McDonald, who oversees Gerringong and its surrounding towns, said the popularity of Tasman Drive had caught the authorities off guard. But he said they were working to address residents’ concerns.

item.title: Regional Mediators Rush to Save U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire
item.description: Pakistan and Qatar have dispatched teams to Iran under the looming threat of resumed war, after weeks of diplomacy failed to produce an agreement.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward, Elian Peltier and Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: People walking through an intersection in Tehran beneath a billboard showing the former supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, earlier this month. He was killed in February, and succeeded by his son.

item.title: In West Bank, Latest Victim of Israeli Settler Violence Shocks in a New Way
item.description: A video of a Palestinian family’s dog being savagely beaten has spread widely in the days after the attack.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.media:description: A dog being beaten by a settler in the West Bank village of Atara in May.

item.title: Before Europe Anoints Someone to Talk to Putin, It Debates What to Talk About
item.description: Europeans are considering appointing an envoy to Ukraine peace talks with Russia. First, many warn, they need to decide what to ask.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek, Valerie Hopkins and Paul Sonne
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia in Moscow, this month.

item.title: On the Ground in South Sudan: Why Akobo Faces an Ebola Risk
item.description: Hunger and conflict are on the minds of the residents of Akobo, where an outbreak could have devastating consequences.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Ed Ram
item.media:credit: Ed Ram for The New York Times

item.title: Why Islamophobic, Antisemitic and Racist Crimes Are Rising in the UK
item.description: Islamophobic, antisemitic and racist crimes are being fueled by online disinformation, global instability and divisive political rhetoric, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Lizzie Dearden
item.media:credit: Jamie Lashmar/Press Assocation, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Outside a mosque in Peacehaven, England, after an arson attack in October.

item.title: Europeans Remain Wary After Trump Promises to Send Troops to Poland
item.description: President Trump said he would send 5,000 troops to Poland, part of a back-and-forth that has left European leaders reeling.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: U.S. Army soldiers before a live-fire exercise in Drawsko Pomorskie, Poland, last year.

item.title: New Taliban Decree on Divorce Formalizes Child Marriage in Afghanistan, U.N. Warns
item.description: A new Afghan law requires girls to wait until puberty before seeking to get out of a marriage. It also requires mediation for women seeking to escape an abusive husband.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Kiana Hayeri for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pharmacy in Kabul, Afghanistan, on Tuesday. The Taliban government has imposed some of the world’s toughest restrictions on women and girls.

item.title: From General to Governor: Putin Pushes a ‘New Elite’ of War Veterans
item.description: The popular leader of a region bordering Ukraine is replaced by a military commander as President Vladimir V. Putin looks to reward those who fought in his war.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:description: A photo released by Russian state media showed President Vladimir V. Putin meeting with Alexander Shuvayev in Moscow this month on the day Mr. Shuvayev was appointed to lead the Belgorod region.

item.title: Manhunt Underway in Philippines for Duterte Ally Who Is Evading I.C.C. Warrant
item.description: The fugitive, Senator Ronald dela Rosa, had sought refuge in the country’s Senate last week, eventually giving government agents the slip.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee
item.media:description: Senator Ronald “Bato” dela Rosa answering questions at the Philippine Senate on May 13. He fled the building early the following day.

item.title: Police Investigation of Former Prince Andrew Examines Reports of Sexual Misconduct
item.description: A British police force said it was assessing a report that a woman had been taken to an address in Windsor in 2010 for sexual purposes. No charges have been filed.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, formerly known as Prince Andrew, attending a service at St. George’s Chapel, Windsor Castle, last year.

item.title: Xi Calls for All-Out Rescue After Coal Mine Explosion Kills at Least 90 in China
item.description: The death count rose drastically on Saturday as the Chinese leader, Xi Jinping, called for an investigation and emphasized the need to “hold those responsible to account.”
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:description: Rescuers at the site of a deadly explosion at the Liushenyu coal mine in China’s Shanxi Province, on Saturday.

item.title: Once Trump’s Co-Pilot Against Iran, Netanyahu Is Now a Mere Passenger
item.description: A partner in the war, Israel has been largely left out of the peace talks, a humbling setback for its prime minister with significant risks for the country.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel with President Trump at Mar-a-Lago in December.

item.title: Mob Burns Congo Ebola Center Amid Rare Strain Outbreak
item.description: Several hundred people massed at the gates of a hospital to demand the body of a suspected Ebola victim. Violence broke out when staff refused the request.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: Deadly Measles Outbreak Sickens Thousands of Children in Bangladesh
item.description: The outbreak, with more than 8,000 confirmed cases and another 60,000 suspected infections, has prompted an emergency vaccination drive.
item.dc:creator: Saif Hasnat and John Yoon
item.media:description: The health authorities in Bangladesh have confirmed more than 8,000 cases of measles in the outbreak.

item.title: A New Era of Exploring the Universe in Radio
item.description: With 263 antennas spread across the U.S. and Mexico, the Next Generation Very Large Array, would join a new wave of radio astronomy.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller

item.title: In Jab at Trump, Mexico Targets Foreign Election Meddling
item.description: Mexico is moving to add protections from “foreign interference” in elections. The rules seem aimed at Washington, but they raised concerns about Mexico’s democracy.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:description: Voters casting ballots in Mexico City during Mexico’s general election in 2024.

item.title: Iran and Oman in Talks Over Strait of Hormuz Ship Payment System
item.description: The discussions suggest that the United States and the Iranian government may not be close to reaching a deal to end a war that has badly damaged the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni, Vivian Nereim, Erika Solomon and Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ships and tankers in the Strait of Hormuz last month.

item.title: Cuba Celebrates Raúl Castro After the U.S. Accuses Him of Murder
item.description: Cuban officials closed ranks around Mr. Castro, their embattled former president, who was indicted on murder charges in the downing of two civilian planes 30 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:description: Former Cuban President Raúl Castro, center, at May Day celebrations in Havana earlier this month.

item.title: Alberta Will Vote on a Possible Referendum to Separate From Canada. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: Premier Danielle Smith announced that she would ask citizens to vote on whether they want to stay in Canada, or hold a referendum to secede.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith at a news conference in Calgary on May 22, 2026.

item.title: Cuban Pilot in Florida Is a Defendant in Raúl Castro Indictment
item.description: The pilot, Luis González-Pardo, was one of the defendants in the indictment that included former Cuban President Raúl Castro.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.media:description: Lt. Col Luis Raúl González-Pardo, left, and Lorenzo Alberto Pérez-Pérez, both MiG pilots in the Cuban Air Force, in an undated photo included in the federal indictment against former Cuban President Raúl Castro.

item.title: In Greenland, Protesters Denounce Opening of Bigger U.S. Consulate
item.description: “Go away!” demonstrators yelled outside the newly opened diplomatic outpost, as guests inside nibbled on musk ox hot dogs.
item.dc:creator: Maya Tekeli and Jeffrey Gettleman
item.media:credit: Oscar Scott Carl/Ritzau Scanpix, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Greenlanders protesting the new American Consulate in Nuuk, Greenland, on Thursday.

item.title: Britain vs. Bonds
item.description: Bond markets have been putting fear into the hearts of politicians for a long time. Britain has become the latest case study.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Turkish Court Ruling Means New Trouble for Erdogan’s Foes
item.description: A surprise judgment replaced the leader of Turkey’s main opposition party with a former leader known for losing elections.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard and Safak Timur
item.media:description: Opposition party members in Turkey’s capital, Ankara, protesting a court ruling that changed their party’s leadership on Thursday.

item.title: U.N. Official Who Says U.S. Punished Her for Speech Wins a Round in Court
item.description: The government has appealed a judge’s ruling that it violated the free speech rights of Francesca Albanese, who was sanctioned for speaking out against Israel.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: Francesca Albanese in Geneva in September.

item.title: Exxon Is Nearing a Deal to Pump Oil in Venezuela, Marking a Victory for Trump
item.description: The U.S. oil giant is negotiating a deal to pump oil in Venezuela, ending a standoff with its socialist rulers and marking a victory for President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil tanker docked at the Cardón refinery in Punto Fijo, Venezuela, in January.

item.title: Germany Charges Two Men With Plotting to Kill Jewish Leaders on Iran’s Behalf
item.description: European authorities have grown concerned about a rise in Iranian intelligence activity in Europe in the wake of the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Iranian Embassy in Berlin in 2024.

item.title: Queen Elizabeth Pushed for Ex-Prince Andrew to Get Envoy Position, New Documents Show
item.description: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor is accused of sharing secrets with Jeffrey Epstein when he was a British trade envoy. Newly released documents shed some light on how he got the appointment.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, formerly Prince Andrew, at Windsor Castle last month.

item.title: Uganda Restricts Travel with Congo Over Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Uganda said that flights to and from the Democratic Republic of Congo would be temporarily suspended, as the outbreak appeared to spread to a rebel-controlled province in Congo.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A health worker measures the temperature of a motorcyclist passing through a checkpoint in Goma, North Kivu, Democratic Republic of the Congo, on Thursday.

item.title: Air France Flight to U.S. Is Diverted to Montreal Over Congolese Passenger Amid Ebola Fears
item.description: An Air France plane was sent to Montreal because a passenger from the Democratic Republic of Congo was on board. The U.S. has closed its borders to recent visitors to the African country.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg

item.title: Trump Again Says He Will Talk to Taiwan’s Leader, Risking China’s Anger
item.description: President Lai Ching-te of Taiwan said he would be happy to speak with President Trump, a move that would defy U.S. diplomatic protocol and infuriate China.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon, Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.media:description: President Trump said on Wednesday that he would talk to Taiwan’s president about a possible arms sale.

item.title: Samsung Averts a Walkout With Big Bonuses, but Discord Over A.I. Profits Brews
item.description: Samsung Electronics, a global memory chip supplier, has been a major beneficiary of the A.I boom. It has also become the center of a debate over how to divide its spoils.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:description: Outside the Samsung Electronics semiconductor plant in Pyeongtaek, South Korea, on Thursday.

item.title: Fire Destroys Sacred Buddhist Hall in Japan that Housed ‘Eternal Flame’
item.description: The flame, which spiritual leaders say has been continuously lit for more than 1,200 years, was salvaged after the blaze and moved to a different site.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:credit: Makoto Kondo/The Yomiuri Shimbun, via Reuters

item.title: In Berlin, Cars Are a Culture War Flashpoint
item.description: With municipal elections approaching, conservatives and progressives are sparring over congestion in the city center and how to fix it.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Tatiana Firsova
item.media:description: Morning traffic in Berlin in March.

item.title: A Powerful El Niño Is Forming. If History Is a Guide, It Could Hit Hard.
item.description: The biggest episodes of the past have altered the course of human events, according to researchers. An emerging one is drawing historic comparisons.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: An illustration published in 1877 depicted a scene in southern India. Millions died from a famine that coincided with an El Niño that year.

item.title: The Hard-Line Military Fraternity Running Iran
item.description: Decision making in the country is guided by a small group of men associated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran showing the three supreme leaders, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomenei, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his son, Mojtaba Khamenei.

item.title: Mira Nair Finds a New Audience as Mother of Zohran Mamdani
item.description: “I feel like we have given him to the world,” the filmmaker said in a conversation with The New York Times. Her next movie is about one of India’s greatest artists.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:credit: Atul Loke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mira Nair, the acclaimed film director and mother of Zohran Mamdani, in Amritsar, India, in March.

item.title: China’s Endless Housing Crisis Shows Faint Signs of Hope
item.description: Property prices in Shanghai, in particular, are rebounding, but the national market still faces an enormous overhang — 90 million empty or unfinished apartments.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:description: Many families in China poured their savings into apartments once seen as a safe path to wealth, only to watch values unravel.

item.title: How China’s Housing Crisis Has Global Consequences
item.description: Our Beijing bureau chief, Keith Bradsher, describes how China’s enormous housing crash is affecting economies all over the world.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher, Rebecca Suner, Thomas Vollkommer, Joey Sendaydiego and Luke Piotrowski

item.title: In Blackout-Hit Cuba, Word of U.S. Castro Indictment Spreads Slowly
item.description: While many Cubans were divided over the legitimacy of the U.S. charging Raúl Castro with murder, the hope for developments that might ease their suffering is widespread.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole González for The New York Times
item.media:description: A furniture workshop in Havana carries reminders that the Castro family has been part of Cuba’s ruling elite for more than 65 years.

item.title: Autocracy’s ‘Loyal Losers’
item.description: Even the most capable autocrats need help hanging on to power. Mediocre officials are often best for the job, according to new research.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Ebola Containment Efforts May Have Been Hindered by USAID Shutdown and CDC Cuts
item.description: Aid cuts by the Trump administration have shut down crucial disease surveillance networks and medical supply chains in East Africa.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Stephanie Nolen
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Jospin Mwisha/Agence France-Presse via Getty Images
item.media:description: Monitoring visitors in Goma, in the Democratic Republic of Congo, where Ebola cases have been confirmed, on Monday.

item.title: Cuba Says the Trump Administration Is Not Negotiating in Good Faith
item.description: Cuba’s ambassador to the United Nations said in an interview that Havana wanted to talk but the Trump administration was creating pretexts for military action.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:description: Ernesto Soberón Guzmán, Cuba’s ambassador to the U.N., in New York on Wednesday.

item.title: Rubio Moves Closer to Decades-Long Goal of Transforming Cuba
item.description: The secretary of state is the son of immigrants who left Cuba before the Castro revolution to look for economic opportunities. Yet, he pursues change with the zeal of a political exile.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Marco Rubio, the secretary of state and a former senator, is a first-generation American citizen whose parents immigrated from Cuba.

item.title: Why the U.S. Chose May 20 to Seek Charges Against Raúl Castro
item.description: The choice of this specific date to indict the former president Raúl Castro carries historical echoes from more than a century ago.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak
item.media:description: A wall in Havana featuring political leaders including the former Cuban president Raúl Castro.

item.title: After Eurovision Final, Dismay That Politics Didn’t Play a Bigger Role
item.description: Politics isn’t supposed to influence the international song contest, but some people wanted relationships between countries to hold more sway in this year’s voting.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Martin Meissner/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Martin Meissner
item.media:description: Alexandra Capitanescu representing Romania in the Eurovision Song Contest last week.

item.title: Justice Dept. Charges Raúl Castro as Trump Escalates Pressure Campaign Against Cuba
item.description: The indictment was an extraordinary escalation of the Trump administration’s multifaceted pressure campaign against Cuba’s Communist government.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer, Frances Robles and David C. Adams
item.media:description: The indictment of Raúl Castro, the former president of Cuba, comes as the country’s oil supplies for domestic use and power plants has been exhausted.

item.title: Video Shows Itamar Ben-Gvir Taunting Pro-Palestinian Activists
item.description: The national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, posted a video in which he is seen with handcuffed activists forced to kneel on the deck of a ship. It drew a rebuke from Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: Israel’s national security minister, Itamar Ben Gvir, in the Israeli parliament, on Wednesday.

item.title: Ebola Crisis Sparks Debate Over Global Health Double Standards
item.description: To some Africans, the claim that the continent’s largest health agency had already bungled its response scratched a familiar wound.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Red Cross workers carry the coffin of a person who died of Ebola at a health center in Rwampara, the Democratic Republic of Congo, on Wednesday.

item.title: Soccer Game Between North and South Korean Teams Draws Strong Emotion On and Off the Pitch
item.description: While diplomatic relations between the two Koreas are near a historic low, a rare trip by athletes from the North triggered intense emotions in some older South Koreans.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Chung Kyung-jo, center, waving, supported the visiting team in the match between two Korean teams, in Suwon, South Korea on Wednesday.

item.title: Australian Swimmer Who Crashed Beach Fashion Show Becomes the Star
item.description: David Handley was going for a swim in Sydney when he found himself walking on an Australian Fashion Week runway. “I’ve usurped the spot of the lead model,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
